docs.title: Super Individuals CIC
docs.text:
Rolling enrolment

SICIC Agent Building Intro-Track

Build your 24/7 AI agent infrastructure in four weeks, then turn it into a public portfolio, a live demo, and a path into founding membership.

Enrolment Rolling · join any week Format Online, hands-on workshop Outcome Live agent, documentation, and GitHub Pages portfolio Price From GBP 86 · Standard GBP 199 Apply for Intro-Track View Programme

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text:
• A deployed agent running 24/7 on your own VPS infrastructure
  • A documented security architecture with sandbox isolation and identity controls
  • A domain-specific sub-agent with custom identity, skills, and workflow logic
  • A public GitHub portfolio showcasing your methodology and agent design

This is not a course about AI concepts. It is a build environment for people who want working systems, not just familiarity with tools.

docs.title: Why This Is Different
docs.text:
Not a course. An incubator.

You are not watching videos. You are building infrastructure, publishing outputs, and learning by shipping.

Invitation-only, referral-aware

Admission is curated. The workshop is the gateway into a deeper membership layer and the path toward founding status.

Portfolio as proof

Participants leave with a live agent, documented method, and a public GitHub Pages site that shows what they built.

Founding cohort path

The strongest early builders can earn their place in the first 31 founding members, with lifetime access and founding-only privileges.

docs.title: Live Now
docs.text:
Intro-track

Rolling enrolment

The intro-track now runs on rolling enrolment, so you can join any week and work through the four-week build path. Pricing starts at GBP 86, with standard admission at GBP 199.

Explore the programme

Founding Cohort

First membership tier

The founding cohort is the first and most limited membership tier: 31 members, lifetime access, and founding-only Discord status and channels.

See membership

Member Demos

Build in public

Early founding members are already publishing live pages that show what they built, how they thought, and what their systems actually do.

Browse cohort demos

Get involved

Apply now

Complete the workshop, publish your work, and earn your path into the community and founding cohort consideration. Rolling intro-track enrolment starts from GBP 86.

Apply now

docs.title: What Super Individuals CIC Is
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC is a workshop and membership community focused on building with AI.

We bring together professionals, builders, and curious minds to explore and apply AI in real-world scenarios, then give them a longer-term community in which to keep building.

docs.title: What We Do
docs.text:
• workshops
  • cohort-based learning
  • invitation-only membership
  • project building

docs.title: Our Goal
docs.text:
To help individuals become AI-native builders, not just consumers of knowledge.

We do that through a workshop gateway, followed by a deeper membership layer for people who are invited to continue.

docs.title: Community
docs.text:
Super Individuals is a long-term community built around one idea: that the most valuable thing AI can do is make it possible for individuals, not just institutions, to create and deliver things that genuinely matter.

We are building a curated, invitation-only ecosystem for people who want to keep building with AI over time, not just attend a single workshop and disappear.

docs.title: Membership First
docs.text:
Membership is the long-term layer of Super Individuals. It is designed for people who want an ongoing place to learn, build, stay close to frontier practice, and grow alongside other serious builders across cohorts.

Membership includes:

  • Discord community access as the permanent home for members
  • all future workshops, free, across cohorts and streams
  • Substack member content including research notes, build logs, and internal thinking
  • free Q&A sessions with the founding team and guest practitioners
  • weekly or fortnightly frontier updates on what is actually moving in applied AI
  • member directory access across cohorts
  • product and framework beta access before public release
  • co-facilitator opportunities for members who want to contribute
  • early pathways into Pegasi and Tensorlink opportunities where relevant

docs.title: The Founding Cohort
docs.text:
The Founding Cohort is the first membership group inside Super Individuals. These are the earliest members of the community, recorded permanently as part of its origin.

Founding Cohort membership is:

  • limited to 31 members
  • invitation only
  • lifetime access
  • the lowest membership price this community will ever offer
  • help shape the culture and direction of the community

As the community grows, the founding rate rises and later members join at higher pricing. This is a structural commitment to the people who were here first.

docs.title: Founding-Only Benefits
docs.text:
What founding members get on top
  • Permanent founding member status on record
  • Special founding cohort badge in Discord
  • Private founding-member channel in Discord
  • Closer access to shaping early community culture and direction
  • Priority visibility for future co-facilitator and contribution opportunities
Why it matters
  • Your place remains marked even as the community scales
  • You are part of the first layer of trust inside the network
  • You get earlier access to conversations, experiments, and programme evolution
  • You help establish the tone for everyone who comes after

docs.title: Membership Pricing
docs.text:
First membership tier, 31 seats only

Founding Cohort

GBP 49.99 lifetime

Invitation-only founding membership with additional founder status and Discord benefits.

  • Free access to all future workshops
  • Full member access across the community
  • Special founding cohort badge
  • Private founding Discord channel
  • Permanent founding member status
  • Substack research notes, build logs, and private thinking
  • Q&A sessions, frontier briefings, and beta access
  • Early pathways into Pegasi and Tensorlink opportunities
Invitation Only

Future membership tiers

Coming soon

Additional membership tiers will be announced later as the community grows beyond the founding cohort.

For now, only the Founding Cohort membership price is being offered.

Coming soon

docs.title: How Membership Works
docs.text:
Membership is not open for public payment. It is offered only after a participant completes and passes the Intro Batch workshop, followed by a final review of results.

If invited, the participant receives a private payment link by email.

The Founding Cohort is available only to selected participants from the earliest workshop cohorts, until all 31 places are filled.

docs.title: Contact
docs.text: For collaborations, partnerships, or questions:

docs.title: Contact Routes
docs.text:
• publication@sicic.org

We are open to:

  • academic collaborations
  • industry partnerships
  • guest speakers

docs.title: Partnership Direction
docs.text: Super Individuals CIC is designed to work with institutions, communities, alumni networks, and mission-aligned organizations that want to help domain experts build practical, domain-specific AI capability.

docs.title: Current Context
docs.text: Our first workshop was framed in an Oxford Saïd alumni context, giving the community an early home among thoughtful professionals.

docs.title: Future Partners
docs.text:
We are open to collaborations with:

  • alumni communities
  • universities and executive education programs
  • innovation labs and public-interest organizations
  • founder networks and professional associations
  • media, research, and cultural institutions

docs.title: Partnership Formats
docs.text:
• private workshop cohorts
  • invited talks and salon events
  • co-branded learning series
  • cohort showcases and member demos
  • editorial collaborations and published reflections

docs.title: What Partners Gain
docs.text:
• a credible entry point into domain-specific AI-agent education
  • programming that feels practical rather than hype-driven
  • stronger engagement through cohort-based participation
  • visible outputs that members can share publicly

docs.title: Start a Conversation
docs.text: To explore a workshop, event, or strategic collaboration, contact publication@sicic.org.

docs.title: Privacy Policy
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC ("SICIC", "we", "us", "our")

Last updated: April 2026

docs.title: 1. Who we are
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC is a Community Interest Company registered in England and Wales. We operate an invitation-only AI agent education community and workshop programme.

Our registered contact for data matters is publication@sicic.org.

docs.title: 2. What personal data we collect
docs.text:
We collect and process the following categories of personal data:

  • Membership and application data: name, email address, professional background, LinkedIn profile URL, and responses submitted via our membership application form.
  • Payment data: name and email address associated with payment. Payment card details are processed directly by Stripe Payments UK Ltd and are not stored by SICIC.
  • Community participation data: messages and interactions within our Telegram and Discord community channels, responses to workshop exercises, and workshop attendance records.
  • Technical data: IP addresses and usage logs generated through interaction with our technical infrastructure, including our Telegram bot (Beacon).
  • Website analytics data: page views, usage events, device/browser information, and related identifiers collected through Google Analytics on our website.
  • Research data: with your separate and explicit consent only, anonymised or identified content from community interactions may be used for academic research into human-AI collaboration. See Section 7.

docs.title: 3. Legal basis for processing
docs.text:
We rely on the following lawful bases under UK GDPR:

  • Contract performance (Article 6(1)(b)): processing necessary to administer your membership, deliver workshop content, and manage payments.
  • Legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f)): operating and improving our community platform, ensuring security, and communicating with members about programme updates.
  • Consent (Article 6(1)(a)): where we ask for your agreement before processing, in particular for use of your data in academic research, or for publishing any content featuring you such as spotlights or case studies.
  • Legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c)): where processing is required to comply with UK law, including financial record-keeping.

docs.title: 4. How we use your data
docs.text:
We use your personal data to:

  • process and administer your membership application and payment
  • deliver workshop curriculum and community support
  • communicate with you about SICIC programmes and events
  • operate and maintain the safety and integrity of our community
  • comply with our legal and regulatory obligations
  • conduct internal analysis to improve our programmes

docs.title: 5. Who we share your data with
docs.text:
We do not sell your personal data. We share data only with the following categories of third-party processors, under appropriate data processing agreements:

Processor Purpose Location Stripe Payments UK Ltd Payment processing UK / EU Google LLC Website analytics USA* Telegram Messenger Community platform EU (servers vary) Discord Inc. Community platform USA* Hetzner Online GmbH Cloud infrastructure hosting Germany (EU) OpenRouter Inc. AI model API (bot functionality) USA*

* Where data is transferred outside the UK/EEA, we rely on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or equivalent UK adequacy mechanisms.

docs.title: 6. How long we keep your data
docs.text:
• Membership records: for the duration of your membership, plus 6 years thereafter (UK legal requirement for financial records).
  • Application data for unsuccessful applicants: deleted within 12 months of the application decision.
  • Community interaction data (Telegram and Discord): retained while the community is active; members may request deletion of their contributions, subject to technical limitations of the relevant platform.
  • Payment records: 6 years from transaction date, as required by HMRC.

docs.title: 7. Research use of community data
docs.text:
SICIC conducts and supports academic research into human-AI interaction and organisational behaviour. Community interaction data, including Telegram and Discord messages and workshop participation records, represents a longitudinal dataset of value to this research.

We will only use your data for research purposes if you provide separate, explicit, and informed consent. This consent will be sought through a dedicated consent form prior to any research project commencing, and will clearly describe what data will be used, how it will be anonymised where applicable, who will have access, and the intended publication venue.

You may withdraw your research consent at any time without affecting your membership.

docs.title: 8. Your rights
docs.text:
Under UK GDPR, you have the right to:

  • access the personal data we hold about you
  • rectify inaccurate data
  • erase your data ("right to be forgotten"), subject to our legal retention obligations
  • restrict processing in certain circumstances
  • object to processing based on legitimate interests
  • data portability for data you provided under contract or consent
  • withdraw consent at any time, without affecting prior processing

To exercise any of these rights, contact us at publication@sicic.org. We will respond within one calendar month.

docs.title: 9. Cookies and tracking
docs.text: Our website uses Google Analytics, which may set cookies or similar tracking technologies to help us understand traffic and improve the site. Our community platforms, including Telegram and Discord, and our payment provider (Stripe) may also set cookies or use tracking technologies on their own platforms. Please refer to their respective privacy policies.

docs.title: 10. Changes to this policy
docs.text: We may update this policy from time to time. Where changes are material, we will notify members via our community channels with reasonable notice before the changes take effect.

docs.title: 11. How to complain
docs.text:
If you have concerns about how we handle your personal data, please contact us first at publication@sicic.org. If you remain unsatisfied, you have the right to lodge a complaint with the UK Information Commissioner's Office:

  • ico.org.uk
  • 0303 123 1113

docs.title: 1. Non-refundable nature of membership
docs.text: All SICIC membership fees are non-refundable upon payment. Membership is a lifetime access entitlement that takes effect immediately upon confirmation of payment. By completing payment, the member acknowledges that the membership service has commenced and expressly consents to the waiver of any applicable cancellation right.

docs.title: 2. Basis under UK Consumer Rights Act 2015
docs.text:
Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013, consumers ordinarily have a 14-day right to cancel distance contracts. However, this right does not apply where:

  • the service has been fully performed
  • the consumer has given prior express consent to performance beginning before the cancellation period has expired and acknowledged that the right to cancel will be lost

By proceeding with payment, the member provides such consent and acknowledgement.

docs.title: 3. Pre-purchase evaluation
docs.text: SICIC membership is accessible only through an invitation-only process that requires prior completion of a paid workshop. Members have therefore had a meaningful opportunity to evaluate the community, curriculum, and culture before committing to membership. No refund will be issued on the basis that expectations were not met.

docs.title: 4. Exceptional circumstances
docs.text: SICIC reserves the right to consider refund requests in exceptional circumstances at its sole discretion. Such requests must be submitted in writing to publication@sicic.org within 7 days of payment. The decision of SICIC's directors shall be final.

docs.title: 5. Membership transfer
docs.text: Membership is personal and non-transferable. It may not be assigned, gifted, or sold to another individual.

docs.title: Workshop Refund Policy
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC ("SICIC")

Last updated: April 2026

docs.title: 1. General principle
docs.text: Workshop fees are non-refundable once the workshop has commenced. Prior to commencement, refund eligibility is determined by the notice period given, as set out below.

docs.title: 2. Cancellation by the participant
docs.text:
Notice given before workshop start Refund More than 7 days Full refund 3-7 days 50% refund Less than 3 days No refund After workshop has commenced No refund

Cancellation requests must be submitted in writing to publication@sicic.org. The date of receipt of the written request determines the applicable notice period.

docs.title: 3. Deferral option
docs.text: In lieu of a refund, participants who cancel with at least 3 days' notice may request to defer their place to the next available cohort of the same workshop stream. Deferral is subject to availability and may be exercised once only. Deferred places have no cash value and cannot be transferred to another individual.

docs.title: 4. Cancellation by SICIC
docs.text:
In the event that SICIC cancels a workshop before it commences, participants will receive a full refund or, at their election, a deferred place in the next available cohort. SICIC's liability in such circumstances is limited to the workshop fee paid and does not extend to any travel, accommodation, or other costs incurred by the participant.

SICIC reserves the right to reschedule a workshop with reasonable notice. Rescheduling does not entitle participants to a refund unless the new date is materially inconvenient and no suitable alternative is offered.

docs.title: 5. No-shows
docs.text: Participants who do not attend a workshop without prior written cancellation are not entitled to a refund or deferral.

docs.title: 6. Exceptional circumstances
docs.text: SICIC may consider refund requests outside the above framework in genuinely exceptional circumstances, for example serious illness or bereavement, at its sole discretion. Such requests must be submitted within 7 days of the missed workshop with supporting documentation where appropriate.

docs.title: 7. Consumer rights
docs.text: Where applicable, nothing in this policy affects your statutory rights under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 or the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013.

docs.title: Terms of Service
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC ("SICIC", "we", "us", "our")

Last updated: April 2026

docs.title: 1. About SICIC
docs.text:
Super Individuals CIC is a Community Interest Company registered in England and Wales. We operate an invitation-only AI agent education programme and community ecosystem. Our mission is to support the co-evolution of humans and AI through practical, cohort-based learning.

As a Community Interest Company, SICIC is constituted for community benefit. Membership fees and any surplus revenue are reinvested into programme delivery, research, and community development. Membership does not confer any ownership interest, equity, or profit entitlement in SICIC or any associated entity.

docs.title: 2. Acceptance of Terms
docs.text:
By submitting a membership application, completing payment, or participating in any SICIC workshop or community platform, you agree to be bound by these Terms of Service, our Privacy Policy, and our Membership Refund Policy, each of which is incorporated herein by reference.

If you do not agree to these terms, you should not proceed with membership or participation.

SICIC operates an invitation-only model. Admission requires:

  • receipt of a valid invitation or referral from an existing member or SICIC director
  • completion of at least one SICIC workshop
  • acceptance by SICIC following any applicable review process
SICIC reserves the right to decline any application without providing a reason.
Membership is personal and non-transferable. You may not share, assign, or resell your membership or any associated access credentials.
You represent that the information you provide in your application is accurate and complete. Membership obtained through misrepresentation may be revoked without refund.
Workshop access is granted upon payment of the applicable workshop fee as set out in your invitation or on our booking page at the time of registration.

Workshop content, including curriculum materials, recorded sessions, agent frameworks, and code examples, is provided for your personal educational use only. You may not redistribute, resell, publish, or sublicense any workshop content without prior written consent from SICIC.

SICIC workshops involve the use of third-party tools and platforms including but not limited to Telegram, Discord, Hetzner VPS, OpenRouter, and n8n. Your use of these platforms is governed by their respective terms of service. SICIC is not responsible for any changes, outages, or policy updates affecting these third-party services.
Workshop schedules, curriculum, and technical requirements may be updated at SICIC's discretion. We will provide reasonable notice of material changes.

SICIC operates community platforms on Telegram and Discord. Access to these platforms is a privilege of membership and may be revoked at SICIC's discretion. SICIC may migrate, consolidate, or add community platforms over time; members will be notified in advance of any such transitions and provided with reasonable onboarding support.

Members agree not to:

  • share API keys, credentials, server access details, or any security-sensitive information in community channels
  • publish, reproduce, or distribute content created by other members without their explicit consent
  • engage in harassment, discrimination, or conduct that undermines the safety or inclusivity of the community
  • use community channels for unsolicited commercial promotion
  • attempt to reverse-engineer, scrape, or systematically extract community content

Security incidents, including accidental sharing of credentials, must be reported immediately to SICIC via direct message to a director or via publication@sicic.org. Swift reporting will be treated as a positive demonstration of community responsibility.
SICIC reserves the right to remove any member from community platforms who breaches these conduct standards, with or without prior warning depending on the severity of the breach.

docs.title: 6.1 SICIC content
docs.text: All curriculum materials, workshop recordings, documentation, agent frameworks, brand assets, and other content produced by SICIC or its directors remain the intellectual property of SICIC. No licence is granted beyond personal educational use.

docs.title: 6.2 Community contributions
docs.text: Where you contribute content to community channels, discussion threads, or co-created resources, you grant SICIC a non-exclusive, royalty-free licence to use, reference, and reproduce that content for internal programme improvement and, with your separate consent, for research or publication purposes.

docs.title: 6.3 Feedback
docs.text: If you provide feedback, suggestions, or ideas about SICIC's programmes, you agree that SICIC may use such feedback without restriction or compensation.

SICIC provides education on the design and deployment of AI agents. We do not warrant that any technique, framework, or tool taught in our workshops will produce specific outcomes, be free from errors, or remain current as AI technology evolves.

Members are solely responsible for any agents, automations, or systems they build and deploy. SICIC accepts no liability for any direct, indirect, or consequential loss arising from the use of skills, code, or frameworks introduced in our workshops.

AI-generated content encountered during workshops, whether from large language models, agents, or automated pipelines, may contain inaccuracies. Members are responsible for independently verifying any output before acting on it in a professional or commercial context.
All fees are payable in GBP via the payment method specified at checkout, currently processed by Stripe Payments UK Ltd.
Workshop fees and membership fees are non-refundable upon payment, as further described in our Membership Refund Policy.
SICIC reserves the right to update its fee structure at any time. Changes will not affect fees already paid.

SICIC may suspend or terminate your membership if you:

  • breach these Terms of Service
  • engage in conduct that SICIC reasonably determines to be harmful to the community
  • provide false information in your application
  • fail to maintain the standard of engagement expected of members

Upon termination, your access to all community platforms including Telegram and Discord, and to workshop materials, will be revoked. Membership fees already paid will not be refunded, except at SICIC's sole discretion in exceptional circumstances.
You may voluntarily withdraw from the community at any time by notifying publication@sicic.org. Withdrawal does not entitle you to a refund.

docs.title: 10. Limitation of Liability
docs.text:
To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, SICIC's total liability to any member for any claim arising out of or in connection with these terms shall not exceed the total fees paid by that member to SICIC in the twelve months preceding the claim. SICIC shall not be liable for any indirect, incidental, consequential, or reputational loss.

Nothing in these terms excludes liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, fraud, or any other liability that cannot be excluded under English law.

docs.title: 11. Governing Law and Disputes
docs.text:
These Terms of Service are governed by the laws of England and Wales. Any dispute arising from or in connection with these terms shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.

Before initiating any formal dispute, members agree to first contact SICIC at publication@sicic.org to attempt resolution in good faith.

docs.title: 12. Changes to These Terms
docs.text: SICIC may update these Terms of Service from time to time. Where changes materially affect members' rights or obligations, we will provide reasonable advance notice via our community channels before the updated terms take effect. Continued participation after that date constitutes acceptance of the revised terms.

docs.title: 13. Contact
docs.text:
For any questions regarding these terms, please contact:

Super Individuals CIC publication@sicic.org

docs.title: WorkshopsSICIC Agent Building Intro-Track
docs.text:
Rolling enrolment

Build your 24/7 AI agent infrastructure in four weeks.

Enrolment Rolling · join any week Format Online, hands-on workshop Duration 4 weeks, around 5 hours per week Price From GBP 86 · Standard GBP 199 Apply Now View Programme

docs.title: What You'll Build
docs.text:
By the end of this programme, you will have:

  • A deployed agent running 24/7 on your own VPS infrastructure
  • A documented security architecture with sandbox isolation and identity controls
  • A domain-specific sub-agent with custom identity, skills, and workflow logic
  • A public GitHub portfolio showcasing your methodology and agent design

This is not a course about AI concepts. It is a workshop where you build real infrastructure and leave with running systems.

docs.title: Who Should Apply
docs.text:
Domain Experts

Who want to productize their methodology and operate at scale without hiring a team.

Non-Technical Professionals

In law, finance, consulting, climate, or operations who already use AI daily but need to move from consumption to control.

Technical Professionals

Who want to understand the orchestration layer and build agentic systems, not just scripts.

Entrepreneurs and Solo Operators

Building company-of-one models who need leverage without headcount.

You do not need a coding background. You need clarity about what you want your agent to do, and the discipline to complete four weekly milestones.

docs.title: Programme Structure
docs.text:
Week 1

Deploy

Get your agent live. By the end of Week 1, your agent is running on a VPS, connected to Discord, and responding to your commands.

Key deliverables
  • VPS setup and ZeroClaw installation
  • API key configuration and cost controls
  • Discord bot deployment with allowlist security
  • First automated task: scheduled reminder via cron

Week 2

Secure and Upgrade

Make it yours, then give it a soul. Introduce security architecture, write your agent's identity files, and build a domain-specific sub-agent.

Key deliverables
  • Sandbox configuration with forbidden paths enforcement
  • Allowlist-based identity control and audit logging
  • Root password and API key rotation
  • Workflow Design Document (200 to 400 words)
  • Nova sub-agent deployment with custom identity
  • MCP tool integration and route registration

Week 3

Document

Turn code into portfolio. Build your knowledge base, deploy a static site, and publish to GitHub Pages.

Key deliverables
  • Obsidian vault with structured documentation
  • Sanitized configuration files
  • MkDocs Material site with public manifesto
  • Live GitHub Pages URL as your proof of work

Week 4

Support and Presentation

Consolidate your learning, troubleshoot final blockers, and present your agent to the cohort. Showcase what you've built and learn from peer implementations.

Key deliverables
  • Final troubleshooting and optimisation
  • Portfolio review and refinement
  • Agent presentation to cohort (5-minute showcase)
  • Peer feedback and knowledge sharing

docs.title: Weekly Live Sessions
docs.text:
Every Saturday, 14:00 London Time 60-minute live Q&A with programme directors Yiju Jia and Dr. Bijun Li.

Ask questions, troubleshoot blockers, and discuss industry use cases.

Rolling onboarding: join the next Saturday session for programme overview, tool stack walkthrough, and community guidelines.

All sessions are recorded. Async participation is supported via Discord.

docs.title: What Makes This Different
docs.text:
Not a course. An incubator.

You are not watching videos. You are building infrastructure alongside 60 peers from six continents. Every participant leaves with a running agent and a documented methodology.

Invitation-only, referral-based

Access is controlled. Pricing tiers reflect network positioning: Co-Founding Member referral (GBP 86), alumni network referral (GBP 139), or standard admission (GBP 199).

Lifetime membership

This is not a one-time workshop. Complete the programme and you gain permanent access to the community, future workshops, and priority listing rights on the Pegasi agent trading platform.

Path to Co-Founding status

Only 31 Co-Founding Member seats exist. 15 are filled. The remaining 16 go to the earliest applicants who complete all four milestones. Co-Founding Members receive lifetime free workshop access and referral power.

31 Founding member candidates 6 Continents 4 Weeks 15 Personalised projects

docs.title: Programme Directors
docs.text:
Yiju Jia

Founding Director, Super Individuals CIC

Oxford Saïd Business School alumna. Background in AI-native product design and organizational behavior research. Co-initiated the Super Individual framework with a focus on domain expert empowerment and agent-to-agent economies.

Dr. Bijun Li

Co-Founding Director, Super Individuals CIC

PhD in Cybersecurity. Serial entrepreneur in Web3 and AI infrastructure. Built AI-driven deal sourcing engines for early-stage VC. Leads the programme's security architecture and cost education modules.

docs.title: Pricing and Membership Tiers
docs.text:
Tier Price Benefits Co-Founding Member Referral GBP 86 Referred by one of 31 Co-Founding Members. Path to lifetime free workshop access if you complete all milestones. Alumni Network Referral GBP 139 Referred by Oxford Saïd or CEIBS network member. Early Bird pricing. Path to Co-Founding status via completion. Standard Admission GBP 199 Open application. Path to Co-Founding status via completion. Lifetime membership regardless of tier.

All tiers include:

  • 4-week hands-on workshop with live weekly Q&A
  • Lifetime community access on Discord
  • All technical guides and documentation
  • Permanent eligibility for future workshops at member rates

Co-Founding Member benefits (earned via completion):

  • Lifetime free access to all future workshops
  • Referral power with the GBP 86 tier for your invitees
  • Priority listing on the Pegasi agent platform
  • Founding Member honour board placement

docs.title: Tools and Technologies
docs.text:
ZeroClawLightweight agent framework, Rust-based and self-hosted Hetzner VPSCloud infrastructure, around GBP 5 per month OpenRouterUnified API access to Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini DiscordCommunity platform and daily communication ObsidianPersonal knowledge management and memory layer n8nWorkflow automation and task orchestration MkDocs MaterialStatic site generation for documentation GitHub PagesPublic portfolio hosting

No prior DevOps or infrastructure experience required. All setup is guided step by step.

docs.title: Frequently Asked QuestionsRolling enrolment. Join any week.
docs.text:
Q: I'm not technical. Can I still do this?

Yes. Approximately 40% of Cohort 1 participants had no prior VPS or coding experience. The setup is guided, and you have weekly live Q&A plus community support.

Q: How much time does this require?

Plan for 5 to 7 hours per week: around 2 hours for the weekly module, around 2 hours for iteration and troubleshooting, around 1 hour for documentation, and 1 hour for the live Q&A.

Q: What if I can't attend the live sessions?

All sessions are recorded. The programme is designed for async participation. Most work happens individually on your own schedule.

Q: What happens if I don't complete all four milestones?

You can roll into a future cohort at a discounted price to complete the programme.

Q: Can I get a refund?

Refunds are available within 7 days of payment if you have not accessed any programme materials. After that, no refunds, but you can defer to a future cohort.

Q: Is this affiliated with Oxford University?

Super Individuals CIC is not directly affiliated with Oxford University. The programme is delivered independently and co-initiated by alumni from Oxford Saïd Business School and Cambridge Judge Business School.

Ready to Build?

60 seats. 16 Co-Founding Member slots available.

Apply Now Contact Us

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Our cohorts focus on building real AI applications. Participants work on projects involving AI agents, automation, and practical use cases.

docs.title: Founding Cohort
docs.text:
The founding cohort is the first membership group inside Super Individuals. These early members are building in public, shipping real work, and establishing the tone for what the community becomes.

Projects are built within a limited timeframe. The focus is on execution, not perfection.

docs.title: Bruno's Bricolage Lab
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Bruno Chikeka
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

Bruno's Bricolage Lab

An agentic system for assembling free-tier resources into working outcomes across domains, with a live protocol, specialist agents, and a public troubleshooting record.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Bruno Chikeka

Systems Architect | AI x Infrastructure x Institutional Design | MSc Oxford Said

System

Bricolage

A domain-agnostic protocol that helps agents discover, select, and combine free resources into working builds.

Architecture

6 Specialist Agents

Master, planner, guide, assembler, discovery, and infra coordinate through a runtime-agnostic MCP layer.

docs.title: Why Bruno Built This
docs.text:
The live demo starts with a concrete problem: free-tier services give individuals enough infrastructure to build real products, but most people do not know which parts still exist, which ones still work, or how to combine them reliably.

Bricolage is Bruno's answer to that problem.

The site frames the project as owned infrastructure rather than rented tooling. The goal is not just to use services, but to assemble them into repeatable outcomes through protocol, verification, and agent coordination.

docs.title: What Bricolage Is
docs.text:
The published site describes Bricolage as a domain-agnostic protocol for assembling free resources into outcomes.

The current implementation includes:

  • 6 agents: master, planner, guide, assembler, discovery, infra
  • 411 free resources in the compute domain
  • 5 assembly patterns: blog-ai, portfolio, saas-starter, ai-chatbot, api-backend
  • 10 MCP tools for catalog browsing, status tracking, and encrypted token storage

The site also makes the underlying standard explicit:

  • every resource has a durability classification
  • every resource carries a verified_at date
  • stale entries can be excluded by the planner

That is a stronger design choice than treating free tiers as static documentation. It turns the resource layer into something agents can reason about.

docs.title: Lead Agents
docs.text:
The master is the entry point and routes the goal to five specialists.

The live site lists these roles clearly:

  • master: reads the goal, picks the domain, delegates
  • planner: reads the catalog, picks the minimal set of resources
  • guide: walks the user through account creation and token capture
  • assembler: takes the plan plus acquired access and builds the outcome
  • discovery: scaffolds a new domain when a goal fits nowhere
  • infra: deploys the runtime onto user-provisioned free compute

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
Bruno's project stands out because it treats free-tier infrastructure as a moving system that needs governance, not just a list of links.

The live demo is strong for three reasons:

  • the protocol is explicit
  • the architecture is decomposed into specialist agents
  • the troubleshooting journal is part of the product record

This is a useful founding cohort pattern: publish the methodology, expose the architecture, and document the failure modes alongside the wins.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text:
• Open Bruno's Bricolage Lab
  • View the source repository

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Bricolage is not a narrow single-agent utility. It is an attempt to build a reusable protocol for turning fragmented free infrastructure into deployable systems. That is exactly the kind of systems-level thinking a founding cohort page should make visible.

docs.title: ZeroClaw Agents
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Gemma Oldfield
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

My Digital Lab

Built on the ZeroClaw framework, this is Gemma Oldfield's space for designing, testing, and evolving agent-driven workflows that turn thinking into action.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Gemma Oldfield

Masters AI for Business Oxford | MA | Software Delivery | Project Delivery | Programme Management | Change Maker | Product Delivery | Scrum Master | University Creative Industries Advisory Board Member

Type

Digital Lab

A working project space for agent architecture, workflow design, and iteration.

Lead Agent

Weeny Jeanie

An AI thought-leadership companion for turning raw thinking into structured social content.

docs.title: Why Gemma Built This
docs.text:
In the AI era, Gemma does not just want to use tools. She wants to build and own intelligent systems.

This lab is where she designs, tests, and evolves agent-driven workflows that turn thinking into action.

It documents:

  • the architecture behind her AI agents
  • the workflows that power them
  • everything that broke along the way

Because the real value is not only in what works. It is also in understanding why it did not.

docs.title: Weeny Jeanie: Thought Leadership Companion
docs.text:
Gemma's primary agent is an AI thought-leadership companion designed to turn raw, unstructured thinking into high-quality social content.

What it does:

  • captures ideas via Telegram through voice, text, image, or links
  • transforms unstructured input into clear, structured insight
  • generates multiple LinkedIn-ready post variations
  • refines tone, clarity, and positioning based on intent

docs.title: Core Capability
docs.text:
Weeny Jeanie acts as a personal thinking partner, not just a content generator.

  • identifies the underlying theme such as leadership, AI, or creativity
  • understands audience and intent
  • shapes ideas into credible, human, insight-led content
  • filters and improves thinking before it becomes output

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text: Principle Meaning Human-in-the-loop Quality over automation Insight-first Not just content, but thinking Modular Designed to evolve into multi-agent systems Low-friction Capture ideas instantly, anywhere

docs.title: What This Is
docs.text:
• a thinking engine
  • a content refinement system
  • a foundation for future agent collaboration

docs.title: What This Is Not Yet
docs.text:
• fully autonomous posting
  • multi-platform optimisation
  • long-form content generation

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:
• multi-agent orchestration with specialised roles
  • automated insight filtering and prioritisation
  • integration into real-world business workflows
  • low-cost deployment for education and SME use

docs.title: Navigation Inside the Demo
docs.text:
• Agent Identity
  • Workflow Design
  • Troubleshooting Journal
  • Showcase
  • Closing Thought

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text: This is one of the early public outputs from the founding cohort. It shows what the community is for: not passive learning, but building visible, testable systems and publishing the thinking behind them.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open ZeroClaw Agents

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: This is not just about building an AI tool. It is about creating a system that helps Gemma think better, articulate more clearly, and create consistently.

docs.title: J's ZeroClaw Lab
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Jamaal Linton
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

J's ZeroClaw Lab

A documented zero-to-deployed journey from first server access to a delegation-capable AI agent system running on a low-cost VPS.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Jamaal Linton

Sales Engineer | Datasite

Lead Agent

Nova

A YouTube Intelligence Officer that reviews video links and returns verdict-first briefs through Telegram.

Infrastructure

$6/Month VPS

The live site frames the build as a practical deployment story on low-cost infrastructure rather than a purely conceptual demo.

docs.title: What The Project Is
docs.text:
The homepage opens with a clear transformation:

Four weeks earlier, Jamaal had never SSH'd into a server. The live site now documents a delegation-capable AI agent system running from a low-cost VPS.

That makes the project valuable for two reasons:

  • it is a working agent build
  • it is also a full record of how the build happened

The site describes itself as a personal knowledge base from the Super Individual Founding Workshop, documenting every step, blocker, and breakthrough from zero to deployment.

docs.title: The Journey
docs.text:
The published page breaks the build into a four-week arc:

  • Week 1: bought a Hetzner VPS, installed ZeroClaw, connected Telegram, sent the first command
  • Week 2: tightened permissions, encrypted API keys, set hard spending limits
  • Week 3: built Nova and learned delegation
  • Week 4: published the documentation site

That is a strong pattern for a cohort page because it shows operational progress, not just a polished endpoint.

docs.title: Meet Nova
docs.text:
Nova is framed on the live site as Jamaal's first sub-agent.

Its role is specific:

  • it acts as a YouTube Intelligence Officer
  • it lives in a sandboxed workspace on the server
  • Jamaal sends a video link through Telegram
  • Nova returns a verdict-first brief

The response format is designed around practical decision-making:

  • HIGH ROI or LOW ROI
  • key findings
  • actionable takeaways

That is the right kind of narrow scope for an early agent system. The value is obvious and the workflow is easy to understand.

docs.title: Blockers And Fixes
docs.text:
One of the strongest parts of the site is the troubleshooting honesty. The live page lists concrete failures and the fix applied:

  • YouTube authentication broke, so the build moved from a browser-cookie package to the Apify YouTube MCP server
  • OpenRouter credits ran out mid-task, which forced better management of max_tokens and model routing
  • tool registration mismatched after migration, which required updating Nova's allowed tools to match the new Apify server names

This is useful evidence that the project is not a surface-level demo. It shows real operator learning.

docs.title: Documentation Pattern
docs.text:
The live site also exposes a broader documentation structure:

  • architecture sections for Identity, Soul, Skills, User, and Style
  • configuration reference
  • build journal
  • showcase of best outputs

That means the project is being treated as a maintainable system rather than a one-off experiment.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open J's ZeroClaw Lab

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Jamaal's project is a strong founding cohort example because it demonstrates operational progression clearly: learn the infrastructure, secure the system, delegate to a focused agent, and publish the record of how it all worked.

docs.title: Luis'ZeroClaw Lab
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.text:

An early ZeroClaw lab scaffold with the site structure in place and architecture, troubleshooting, and showcase sections ready to be developed.

The navigation already anticipates core sections for system design, troubleshooting, and outputs.

docs.title: What Is Live Now
docs.text:
The live demo currently exposes a basic MkDocs installation under the title Luis'ZeroClaw Lab.

What is visible today is limited, but there are still a few real signals:

  • the site is framed as a ZeroClaw lab
  • the navigation includes an Architecture section
  • the site structure includes Troubleshooting and Showcase
  • the architecture section is broken into Identity, Soul, and Skills

The homepage content itself is still the default MkDocs starter page, so this project should be treated as an early public scaffold rather than a finished demo.

docs.title: Why That Still Matters
docs.text:
Early-stage pages are still useful when the structure is right.

Luis has already put the core documentation pattern in place:

  • define the agent architecture
  • document what breaks
  • publish outputs when they are ready

That is the correct shape for a serious member lab. The live site is not yet a mature public case study, but it is clearly set up to become one.

docs.title: Current Reading Of The Build
docs.text:
Based on the live page, the most accurate interpretation is:

  • the site title and navigation are customized
  • the architecture information architecture is in place
  • the public-facing content has not yet been populated

So this is best described as a ZeroClaw lab in setup rather than a completed project demo.

docs.title: Visit the Demo

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Not every founding cohort page needs to pretend the project is finished. In Luis's case, the honest state is that the scaffolding is live and the documentation structure is ready. That is still a valid build milestone, and it gives the next iteration a clear place to land.

docs.title: CHIP — Charlie's Padel Coach
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Charlotte Luzuka
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

CHIP — Charlie's Padel Coach

A focused coaching agent that turns match context and short clips into clear, prioritised improvement guidance for padel players.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Charlotte Luzuka

Senior Product Manager | General Operator | Digital Health & Innovation Leader | Angel Investor | Women in Tech Advocate

Product

Padel Coaching Agent

CHIP is designed to diagnose player gaps and reduce overwhelm by narrowing the next actions to what matters most.

Output

4-Week Improvement Plans

The live product turns short inputs into coaching feedback, priorities, and structured practice direction.

docs.title: What CHIP Does
docs.text:
The live site is clear about the product.

CHIP helps players turn matches into actionable improvement by:

  • identifying the biggest performance gaps
  • giving only 3 priority fixes
  • telling the player what to focus on in the next match
  • building structured 4-week improvement plans
  • reviewing short clips with player-provided context

That makes the product narrow in the right way. It is not trying to be a generic sports bot. It is focused on diagnosis, prioritisation, and next-step coaching.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The published workflow is intentionally simple:

  1. Play a match
  2. Send a short description or clip with context
  3. Get clear coaching guidance in seconds

The example prompt on the live page shows the kind of structured context the system expects:

  • level
  • side
  • struggle
  • goal

That is a good product choice. It keeps the input lightweight while giving the system enough signal to produce useful coaching output.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
Charlotte's site frames the problem accurately:

  • most players do not know what they are doing wrong
  • they try to fix too many things at once
  • they do not improve consistently

CHIP responds by focusing on:

  • clarity
  • prioritisation
  • match-based learning

This is a strong founding cohort project because it takes an everyday improvement problem and turns it into a structured, decision-support workflow rather than just a content experience.

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
Beyond the homepage, the live site also shows a solid documentation pattern:

  • Start Here
  • agent architecture sections for Identity, Soul, Skills, User, and Style
  • Journal with Week 4 Notes
  • Showcase with Best Results

That means the project is not only a demo. It is being documented as a system with product thinking behind it.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open CHIP — Charlie's Padel Coach

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: CHIP is a good example of a member build with a clear user problem, a constrained interface, and an output that is immediately useful. The product focus is tighter than most early demos, which makes it easier to see where the value sits.

docs.title: Gp's ZeroClaw Lab
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Gigi Palma
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

Gp's ZeroClaw Lab

A personal agent experiment lab documenting Nova's design, weekly lessons, and an early real estate analyzer showcase.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Gigi Palma

Strategic Program & Transformation Leader | Scaling Global Teams, Risk & Operations | PMP | Oxford MSc

Lead Agent

Nova

The architecture section of the live demo is centered on Nova and the agent identity behind the lab.

Showcase

Real Estate Analyzer

An early public project used to demonstrate how the lab turns agent experimentation into a concrete use case.

docs.title: What The Demo Shows
docs.text:
The live site is positioned as a personal experiment lab rather than a polished product launch.

It explicitly documents three things:

  • Nova agent architecture
  • weekly learnings captured in a journal
  • a real estate analyzer showcase project

That framing matters. The project is not pretending that the interesting part is only the finished output. It treats the system design and the learning process as part of the asset.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: The published demo is organised into three main sections:

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The architecture section is built around Nova and breaks the agent down into:

  • identity
  • soul
  • skills
  • user
  • style

This suggests a deliberate effort to define the agent beyond functionality alone, which is a useful pattern for any member building an owned system with a recognisable behaviour and role.

docs.title: Journal
docs.text: The journal currently exposes weekly learnings, including a Week 3 Lessons entry. That makes the lab a working log of iteration rather than a static portfolio page.

docs.title: Showcase
docs.text: The showcase currently highlights a Real Estate Analyzer project. In the context of the lab, this functions as the proof point for how the underlying agent design can be applied to a concrete domain problem.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
Gp's ZeroClaw Lab is a useful founding cohort example because it documents an agent system at the right level of fidelity:

  • the agent identity is explicit
  • the build process is visible
  • the learning loop is part of the output
  • there is already a clear application example

That is the right direction for a member lab. It turns experimentation into an owned body of work instead of leaving it as a set of disconnected prompts or notes.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open Gp's ZeroClaw Lab

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Gigi's project is still early, but the structure is correct: define the agent, document the lessons, and connect the architecture to a practical showcase. That is the kind of build record that scales as the system becomes more capable.

docs.title: Why Mafalda Built This
docs.text:
In the AI era, Mafalda wants to own assets, not just use tools.

docs.title: Nova
docs.text:
Nova is Mafalda's Content Intelligence Officer.

What Nova does:

  • monitors YouTube business intelligence continuously

docs.title: HackStudio Pro
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Aijie Shu
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

HackStudio Pro

AI-powered video production studio that turns research into broadcast-ready YouTube documentaries in two languages, from a single codebase.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Aijie Shu

AI, Blockchain, Full-Stack Developer

Type

Video Production Studio

A code-driven system for documentary research, scripting, rendering, and bilingual publication.

Stack

Remotion + React 19 + AI

Strict TypeScript, AI-assisted research, MiniMax TTS, and code-driven rendering.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
HackStudio Pro treats video production as a software problem rather than a manual editing workflow.

The published demo shows a system designed to:

  • research across 17+ platforms
  • produce bilingual output in Chinese and English
  • work from a single codebase
  • reduce manual editing dependency
  • validate output before expensive renders

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text:
The site frames the problem directly: making one high-quality documentary takes weeks, doing it in two languages doubles the work, and scaling by hand becomes unrealistic.

HackStudio Pro collapses that workflow by turning each stage of production into code.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
The live demo describes an end-to-end pipeline from raw sources to rendered .mp4, including:

  • concept
  • research
  • script
  • B-roll sourcing
  • editor pass
  • TTS generation
  • build
  • validation harness
  • render

docs.title: Architecture Highlights
docs.text:
According to the live site, some of the key architectural ideas are:

  • scripts are represented as TypeScript data
  • timing is computed from audio rather than manual timeline dragging
  • animations are React components
  • bilingual output is driven from one codebase
  • each narration line has a typed kind that selects the right renderer

docs.title: Auto-director for B-roll
docs.text: The demo includes a video-editor skill that reads the script, scores it against different documentary director profiles, and proposes B-roll choices that feel edited rather than merely assembled.

docs.title: Pre-render validation harness
docs.text:
Before rendering, the system runs five checks to catch avoidable mistakes:

  • counts consistency
  • TTS integrity
  • breathing time
  • B-roll overlap
  • text density

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
The live demo lists a stack that includes:

  • Remotion 4.0
  • React 19
  • TailwindCSS v4
  • TypeScript
  • MiniMax TTS
  • OpenAI Whisper
  • OpenRouter Vision
  • Bun

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text: This is one of the strongest examples so far of a founding cohort member turning AI into owned production infrastructure. It is not just a prompt workflow or a single-purpose bot. It is a full publishing system with research, editorial logic, rendering, and validation built in.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open HackStudio Pro

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: HackStudio Pro shows what it looks like when agentic systems are used not only to automate tasks, but to redesign an entire creative production pipeline around code, structure, and repeatability.

docs.title: Paper Party
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Yiwen (Eavan) Tuo
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

Paper Party

Upload a paper. Discover the conversations. Join the table.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Yiwen Tuo

PhD Candidate | Visiting Researcher @ Trinity College Dublin

Type

Research Conversation Tool

A system for turning uploaded papers into discoverable literature conversations and discussion spaces.

Mode

Upload, Discover, Dialogue

A simple flow from paper upload to mapped literature context and debate.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
Paper Party is designed around a simple research workflow:

  • upload a research paper in PDF form
  • discover the surrounding academic conversation
  • join a discussion table and debate with the literature

docs.title: Core Idea
docs.text: The live demo frames academic reading as something more social and navigable. Instead of treating a paper as an isolated document, the product aims to help users see the surrounding conversation and step into it.

docs.title: Published Flow
docs.text: The current live page presents the project in three stages:

docs.title: Upload
docs.text: Drop a research paper in PDF format.

docs.title: Discover
docs.text: AI maps the literature landscape around the paper.

docs.title: Dialogue
docs.text: Users join tables and debate with the literature.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text: This is a strong founding cohort example because it takes a familiar knowledge task, reading a paper, and reframes it as a structured interactive workflow. The project is not only about summarising content. It is about helping users move from solitary reading to contextualised academic conversation.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open Paper Party

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Paper Party points toward a more collaborative research experience: upload the paper, see the wider discussion, and enter the conversation with more context than a standalone PDF can provide.

docs.title: My ZeroClaw Lab
docs.text: Founding Cohort Demo

docs.title: Jaidev Venkatesan
docs.text:
Founding Cohort

My ZeroClaw Lab

A ZeroClaw-based lab focused on content intelligence, delegation, and owned agent workflows delivered through Telegram.

View Live Demo

docs.title: Project Snapshot
docs.text:
Builder

Jaidev Venkatesan

Senior Manager - Enabling Data & AI Governance

Lead Agent

Nova

A YouTube-focused content intelligence officer that searches, filters, and summarizes relevant videos.

Workflow

Telegram + MCP

ZeroClaw manages delegation while a custom MCP server powers the YouTube retrieval pipeline.

docs.title: Why Jaidev Built This
docs.text:
The live site frames the project clearly: Jaidev wanted to build something he actually owns, not just use someone else's AI tool.

This lab documents both what he built and what went wrong along the way.

docs.title: Nova
docs.text:
Nova is Jaidev's Content Intelligence Officer, focused on YouTube discovery and summarisation.

According to the live demo, Nova:

  • searches YouTube through the Data API v3 via a custom MCP server
  • delivers the top 3 videos with 3-bullet summaries to Telegram
  • supports deeper analysis on request by replying with a number
  • manages topics through /topics commands

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
ZeroClaw acts as the manager. It receives commands through Telegram, delegates YouTube work to Nova, then renders thumbnails and presents the results.

Nova stays focused on content analysis.

The published pipeline is:

  • MCP server
  • Python fetcher
  • YouTube Data API v3

docs.title: Key Numbers
docs.text:
The live site lists the following project details:

Metric Value Framework ZeroClaw v0.3.4 VPS Hetzner, 2 vCPU, 3.7GB RAM Model Claude Haiku 4.5 via OpenRouter Lead agent Nova — YouTube Content Intelligence Topics AI agents, Super Individual business, Claude AI Cost limits $5/day, $20/month Security Workspace sandbox, Telegram allowlist, audit logging Monthly cost Around $6 VPS plus around $5-15 API

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
This is a strong founding cohort project because it turns a common AI use case, content discovery, into an owned operating workflow with clear delegation, controlled topics, and lightweight delivery through Telegram.

It is not just a chatbot. It is a managed content intelligence system with a defined role for the manager, a focused specialist agent, and a custom tool layer underneath.

docs.title: Visit the Demo
docs.text: • Open My ZeroClaw Lab

docs.title: Closing Thought
docs.text: Jaidev's project shows how a small, owned agent system can become useful quickly when the role is narrow, the delegation is clear, and the supporting toolchain is designed around a real workflow.
